quiz.title: Algebra 2: Unit 9 Mixed Review
quiz.subtitle: Unit 9 Quiz 12: Probability & Counting — Challenging Problems (10 Questions)
quiz.subject: Mathematics
quiz.topic: Unit 9: Probability & Counting — High-End Mixed Review

questions.question: A number from 1 to 40 is selected at random. What is the probability it is a multiple of 4 OR a multiple of 6?
questions.explanation: Multiples of 4: 10 numbers. Multiples of 6: 6 numbers. Overlap (multiples of 12): 3 numbers. $P = (10 + 6 - 3)/40 = 13/40$.

questions.question: A survey of 200 people: 120 own a car, 80 own a bike, and 50 own both. Given a person owns a bike, what is the probability they also own a car?
questions.explanation: $P(\text{car} \mid \text{bike}) = \frac{50/200}{80/200} = \frac{50}{80} = \frac{5}{8}$. Of the 80 bike owners, 50 also own a car.

questions.question: A jar has 7 green and 3 yellow candies. A candy is drawn, replaced, and a second candy is drawn. What is the probability both are yellow?
questions.explanation: With replacement, events are independent. $P(\text{both yellow}) = 3/10 \times 3/10 = 9/100 = 0.09$.

questions.question: Given $P(A) = 0.5$, $P(B) = 0.3$, and $P(A \cup B) = 0.65$. Find $P(A \cap B)$ and determine if A and B are independent.
questions.explanation: $P(A \cap B) = 0.5 + 0.3 - 0.65 = 0.15$. $P(A) \cdot P(B) = 0.5 \times 0.3 = 0.15$. They match, so independent.

questions.question: How many ways can 6 people line up if two specific people must stand next to each other?
questions.explanation: Treat the pair as one block: 5 units → $5! = 120$. The pair can swap: $2! = 2$. $120 \times 2 = 240$.

questions.question: A 4-digit code is formed using digits 1-9 with no repetition. The code must start with an odd digit. How many codes are possible?
questions.explanation: First digit: 5 odd choices. Then $8 \times 7 \times 6 = 336$. Total: $5 \times 336 = 1{,}680$.

questions.question: The letters in 'ALGEBRA' are randomly arranged. What is the probability the arrangement starts and ends with 'A'?
questions.explanation: Total arrangements: $7!/2! = 2520$. Fix A's at ends, arrange middle 5: $5! = 120$. $P = 120/2520 = 1/21$.

questions.question: A test has 8 true/false questions. A student guesses on all. What is the probability of getting at most 2 correct?
You can take a guaranteed $\$50$ or play a game: 30% chance of winning $\$200$, 70% chance of winning $\$0$. Which has the higher expected value, and by how much?
questions.explanation: $E(\text{game}) = 200(0.3) + 0(0.7) = \$60$. Guaranteed = $\$50$. Game is higher by $\$10$ in expectation.

questions.question: Two cards are drawn from a 52-card deck without replacement. What is the probability the first is a King and the second is a Queen?

scoreMessages.90: Outstanding! You've mastered Unit 9: addition with overlapping sets, conditional probability, multiplication with and without replacement, independence testing, restricted arrangements, counting with constraints, repeated letter probability, binomial 'at most', expected value decisions, and dependent card draws.
scoreMessages.70: Strong work! Key areas to review: (1) Addition Rule: always subtract the intersection, (2) Conditional probability restricts the denominator to the given event, (3) 'At most k' binomial problems sum P(0) through P(k), (4) Arrangements with repeated letters: divide by the factorial of repetitions.
scoreMessages.50: Good effort on challenging material. Focus on: (1) Finding intersections from unions: $P(A \cap B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cup B)$, (2) Treating a pair as a block in arrangements, (3) When letters repeat, divide total permutations by the factorial of each repeated letter's count, (4) Expected value helps compare risky vs. guaranteed options.
scoreMessages.0: Review Unit 9 fundamentals: (1) $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$, (2) $P(A|B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$, (3) Multiplication: $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B|A)$, (4) Independent if $P(A) \cdot P(B) = P(A \cap B)$, (5) Binomial: $P(k) = C(n,k) p^k q^{n-k}$, (6) Arrangements with repeats: $\frac{n!}{r_1! \cdot r_2!}$.
